findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace 'docs/cli-reference.md — document the command' with 'run `python scripts/gen_cli_reference.py` to regenerate `docs/cli-reference.md` (it is auto-derived from `known_subcommands()`; do not hand-edit)'.
evidence: docs/cli-reference.md line 3: 'Generated by `scripts/gen_cli_reference.py` — do not edit by hand. Run `python scripts/gen_cli_reference.py` to regenerate; a CI drift gate fails the build if this file is stale.'
scripts/gen_cli_reference.py: render() calls _help.known_subcommands() and iterates all help-backed subs automatically
Plan names docs/cli-reference.md as a direct edit target without mentioning scripts/gen_cli_reference.py
findings.finding: `docs/cli-reference.md` is listed as a direct edit target, but it is a generated file produced by `scripts/gen_cli_reference.py` (the file header reads 'Generated by `scripts/gen_cli_reference.py` — do not edit by hand'). For a help-backed subcommand, the generator auto-picks up `metrics` via `known_subcommands()` once `metrics.txt` is added — no hand-edit of the .md is needed. However, the plan does not mention running or updating `scripts/gen_cli_reference.py`, which is the actual driver of the docs update and is CI-drift-gated.
findings.location: Files section / 'docs/cli-reference.md — document the command'
scenarios: An implementer hand-edits docs/cli-reference.md; then CI runs scripts/gen_cli_reference.py --check and fails because the generated output differs from the hand-edited version.
An implementer runs scripts/gen_cli_reference.py, which auto-generates the docs correctly — but the plan's file list doesn't name this script, risking it being skipped.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Files section, replace the `docs/cli-reference.md` entry with: `scripts/gen_cli_reference.py` — no code change needed, but run it to regenerate `docs/cli-reference.md` after adding `metrics.txt`; the CI drift gate (`--check` mode) enforces this.

findings.finding: `docs/cli-reference.md` is auto-generated by `scripts/gen_cli_reference.py` (which has a CI drift gate), but the plan lists it as a direct hand-edit target and omits `scripts/gen_cli_reference.py` from the edit set entirely.
findings.location: Files section: `docs/cli-reference.md — document the command`
findings.suggested_fix: In the Files section, replace the `docs/cli-reference.md` entry with: `docs/cli-reference.md — regenerated (not hand-edited) by running `python scripts/gen_cli_reference.py` after the help file is added; the CI drift gate enforces this.`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend the CLI test) that invokes `rebar metrics --since ... --until ... --output text`, asserts exit 0, and asserts the output contains at least one line matching the described `<id>  [<lens>/<source>]  <value-or-unavailable>` layout.
evidence: '`--output text` is a deterministic human summary — one line per metric in registry order: `<id>  [<lens>/<source>]  <value-or-"unavailable: <reason>">` (a fixed-width, self-defined layout…)'
All five ACs test only `--output json`, `--help`, golden snapshots, portability guard, and `--bucket week` (also JSON) — no AC covers `--output text`.
AC 1 explicitly names `--output json`; no AC names `--output text`.
findings.finding: The `--output text` rendering path is described as a non-deferred deliverable with a specific, named format (one line per metric, fixed-width layout, `unavailable: <reason>` suffix), but no Acceptance Criterion validates it — no AC asserts `rebar metrics --output text` exits 0, produces the described layout, or is tested at all. A non-deferred goal without an end-state-observable proof violates E3(b).
findings.location: Approach section: 'Output formats. … `--output text` is a deterministic human summary — one line per metric in registry order …'; Tests section lists only `--output json` in the CLI test.
scenarios: A developer implements text output incorrectly (e.g., wrong column order, missing `unavailable:` prefix) and no test catches it — the feature ships broken with no observable failure in the AC suite.
findings.suggested_fix:
Add to Tests: 'CLI test also invokes `rebar metrics --since 2026-01-01 --until 2026-07-01 --output text`, asserts exit 0, and asserts stdout contains one line per registered metric id matching the pattern `<id>  [` (checking id and bracket-delimited lens/source prefix).'
Add a corresponding AC: '`--output text` exits 0 and each registered metric id appears on its own line with `[<lens>/<source>]` present.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the bucketed JSON output schema: define the top-level envelope (e.g., `{"buckets": [{"since": "...", "until": "...", "metrics": {...}}, ...]}`) and the bucket key/identifier format so the AC and downstream consumers have a concrete contract.
evidence: Plan: 'For `--bucket`, it builds one context per bucket sub-range and evaluates the registry per bucket, keying the output by bucket'
Plan output-format section defines a flat schema `{"since","until","metrics": {...}}` for the non-bucket case but gives no equivalent for the bucket case
AC: 'the JSON contains at least one bucket key' — no definition of what a bucket key looks like
ABSENCE: no bucket-output schema or key-format definition anywhere in the plan
findings.finding: The `--bucket` JSON output shape is referenced across the plan ('keying the output by bucket', AC: 'the JSON contains at least one bucket key') but the bucket key format and the top-level JSON envelope for a bucketed response are never defined. An implementer does not know whether keys are ISO week-start dates (`2026-01-05`), week-number strings (`week-2`), ISO range strings (`2026-01-01/2026-01-07`), or something else — nor whether the top-level shape is `{"buckets": {"<key>": {"since","until","metrics":{...}}}}` or a flat object or a list.
findings.location: ## Approach — 'For --bucket, it builds one context per bucket sub-range'; ## Acceptance Criteria — last bullet; ## Output formats section
scenarios: Two implementers independently choose different key formats (ISO date vs. ordinal week), producing incompatible outputs that both satisfy the AC.
A downstream consumer of `rebar metrics --bucket week` has no contract to code against.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `evaluate()` (or the loop in `metrics.py`) wraps each reader's `compute` call in a try/except and maps any raised exception to an `unavailable` entry with `reason: "error: <exception type>"`, and add an AC asserting the command exits 0 even when a reader raises.
evidence: Plan says evaluate emits 'value-or-unavailable per metric' but only describes the unavailable path as a data-absence signal, not an exception-catch path
No try/except or error-boundary language around reader invocation in the approach
AC only tests exit 0 on a happy path; no AC for reader exception → graceful degradation
findings.finding: The plan does not specify how `evaluate()` handles a reader that raises an exception (as distinct from returning an `unavailable` signal). If a reader's `compute` callable throws (e.g., git subprocess fails, store file unreadable, unexpected data format), the CLI behavior is undefined — it may crash with a traceback rather than emitting an `unavailable` entry.
findings.location: Approach — 'The context object' / 'A thin CLI over the registry' section; tests/interfaces/test_metrics_cli.py
scenarios: git reader raises CalledProcessError when the git binary is absent
sidecar reader raises FileNotFoundError on a malformed store
one reader raises mid-loop, crashing the entire metrics command instead of marking that metric unavailable
findings.suggested_fix: In the Approach section, add: 'The evaluate loop wraps each `compute(ctx)` call in try/except BaseException; any raised exception is caught and the metric is emitted as `{"unavailable": {"reason": "error: <ExceptionType>", "accruing_since": null}}` — the loop never propagates a reader exception to the caller.' Mirror this with an AC: 'A reader that raises does not crash the CLI; the metric appears as unavailable in the output.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how `metrics.py`'s render loop handles exceptions raised by `compute()` (e.g. catch per-spec and emit as `{"unavailable": {"reason": "compute error: <exc>", ...}}`) so one failing reader cannot crash the entire command and break the AC 'entry for every id' guarantee.
evidence: `evaluate()` in registry.py line 111: `result = spec.compute(context)` — no try/except
`git_metrics._git()` line 41: `subprocess.run([...], check=True, ...)` — raises CalledProcessError on git failure
`git_metrics._read_cap()` line 77: `Path(repo_root / _LIMIT_FILE).read_text(...)` — raises FileNotFoundError if limit file absent
Plan states only: 'emitting value-or-`unavailable` per metric' — no mention of exception handling
findings.finding: The render loop calls `registry.evaluate(spec, context)` which in turn calls `spec.compute(context)` with no exception guard. Several compute callables can raise — `git_metrics` uses `subprocess.run(..., check=True)` (raises `CalledProcessError` if git fails) and `Path.read_text()` (raises `FileNotFoundError` if the cap file is absent); `event_metrics` uses `json.load()` on disk files (raises on corrupt JSON). The plan only specifies the happy-path (value) and None→Unavailable dispatch; it does not specify how a raised exception from `compute()` should be handled. Without an explicit policy, a single failing metric crashes the whole command rather than emitting a per-metric error entry.
findings.location: Approach section — 'The context object (the contract the readers already expect)' / `src/rebar/_commands/metrics.py` render loop
scenarios: rebar metrics run in a repo without .github/module-size-limit.txt: module_size_distribution compute raises FileNotFoundError, crashing the whole command
rebar metrics run outside a git repo or with a broken git binary: churn/refactor_to_addition_ratio raise CalledProcessError
corrupt event JSON file on disk: event_metrics compute raises JSONDecodeError mid-loop
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap each `evaluate()` call in a per-spec try/except and emit an `Unavailable`-shaped entry on any exception, e.g.: `try: result = evaluate(spec, ctx) / except Exception as e: result = Unavailable(reason=f'compute error: {e}', accruing_since=spec.accruing_since)`. Document this policy in the Approach section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the Approach section whether the `metrics.py` render loop catches per-metric `compute` exceptions and folds them into an `unavailable` result (with the exception message as the reason), or lets them propagate — and if the former, document this as part of the `evaluate()` contract.
evidence: Plan: 'it builds a context object and calls `rebar.metrics.registry.evaluate(spec, context)` for every MetricSpec, emitting value-or-`unavailable` per metric'
registry.py `evaluate()` has no try/except — it calls `spec.compute(context)` directly
git_metrics.py uses `subprocess.run(..., check=True)` which raises CalledProcessError on git failure
event_metrics.py reads files with `open(path)` which raises OSError on missing/unreadable files
ABSENCE: no exception-handling protocol specified for the render loop or for evaluate() in the plan
findings.finding: The plan specifies `evaluate()` dispatches on `None`-vs-non-`None` return but never defines what happens when a reader's `compute` callable raises an exception (e.g., `git_metrics` uses subprocess, `event_metrics`/`sidecar_metrics` do file I/O). The CLI render loop in `metrics.py` has no specified exception-handling protocol — the plan neither requires catching and folding into `unavailable` nor explicitly allows crash propagation.
findings.location: ## Approach — 'calls `rebar.metrics.registry.evaluate(spec, context)` for every `MetricSpec`, emitting value-or-`unavailable` per metric'
scenarios: Running `rebar metrics` in a repo with no git history causes `git log` to raise CalledProcessError, crashing the entire command rather than emitting `unavailable` for the affected metrics.
A partially-initialized store missing some ticket dirs causes file I/O errors in event_metrics that crash the command mid-render, leaving partial JSON output on stdout.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Approach: 'The render loop wraps each `evaluate(spec, ctx)` call in a try/except; any exception is folded into `Unavailable(reason=str(exc), accruing_since=spec.accruing_since)` so a single failing reader never crashes the command.'

findings.finding: The plan does not specify whether `registry.evaluate` (or the CLI loop in `metrics.py`) catches exceptions thrown by individual reader `compute` functions and converts them to `unavailable`, or allows them to propagate and crash the entire CLI invocation. If a single misbehaving reader throws an unhandled exception, the user loses all output for all metrics.
findings.location: Approach — 'builds a context object and calls rebar.metrics.registry.evaluate(spec, context) for every MetricSpec, emitting value-or-unavailable per metric'
findings.suggested_fix: In the Approach section, add: 'Each call to a reader's compute is wrapped individually; any exception is caught and emitted as `{"unavailable": {"reason": "reader_error: <exc type>", "accruing_since": null}}` so all other metrics still render.'

findings.finding: The plan specifies that `metrics.py` emits 'value-or-unavailable per metric' for every registered id, but never specifies the fallback when a reader's `compute` callable raises an exception. The existing `registry.evaluate` implementation has no try/except around `spec.compute(context)`, so an exception in any single reader would abort the entire CLI invocation rather than emitting an `unavailable` entry for that metric.
findings.location: ## Approach — context object / emit paragraph; src/rebar/metrics/registry.py:evaluate()
findings.suggested_fix: In the plan's Approach section add: 'If `compute` raises, `metrics.py` (or `evaluate`) catches the exception and yields `Unavailable(reason=str(exc), accruing_since=spec.accruing_since)` — a reader failure is isolated, never fatal.' Then wrap `spec.compute(context)` in a try/except in `evaluate`.

findings.finding: The plan specifies that each metric emits 'value-or-unavailable' but does not define where/how reader exceptions are caught — if a reader raises, it is unspecified whether `evaluate` catches it and converts it to `unavailable`, or whether it propagates and crashes the CLI process.
findings.location: Approach section / AC line: 'each entry being either a value (with source+confidence) or an unavailable object'
findings.suggested_fix: In `registry.evaluate`, wrap each `spec.compute(ctx)` in a try/except, returning `{"unavailable": true, "reason": str(e)}` on failure. Document this contract in the plan's Approach section and add an AC asserting that a reader that raises still yields an `unavailable` entry rather than propagating.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the `--bucket` JSON output schema (e.g. top-level key `"buckets": [{"since": "...", "until": "...", "metrics": {...}}, ...]` or a keyed map `{"2026-01-01/2026-01-08": {"metrics": {...}}, ...}`) and update AC5's assertion to use a concrete `jq -e` path against that schema.
evidence: "For `--bucket`, it builds one context per bucket sub-range and evaluates the registry per bucket, keying the output by bucket."
AC5: 'the JSON contains at least one bucket key' — no jq path or key format given
JSON schema block defines only the flat (no-bucket) format: `{"since","until","metrics": {...}}`; bucketed format is absent
findings.finding: The `--bucket` JSON output format (top-level shape and bucket key format) is never specified. The plan says 'keying the output by bucket' but does not define what the keys look like (ISO period-start date? 'week-1'? an object with 'start'/'end'?). This leaves the executor to invent the schema AND makes AC5's proving command unwritable — you cannot write `jq -e '...'` against an undefined structure.
findings.location: Approach section — 'Output formats' + Acceptance Criteria AC5
scenarios: Executor invents a key format (e.g. `"2026-W01"`) that a downstream consumer or the test harness doesn't expect.
AC5 cannot be expressed as a precise `jq -e` assertion without knowing the top-level key that holds bucket data.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the 'Output formats' section: 'When `--bucket` is supplied, the JSON envelope gains a top-level `"buckets"` array: `[{"since": "<ISO>", "until": "<ISO>", "metrics": {<same per-metric shape>}}, ...]`; `"since"` and `"until"` at the top level reflect the full requested range.' Then rewrite AC5 as: `jq -e '.buckets | length >= 2' <output>` (a two-week range produces ≥2 entries).

evidence: 2 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
vague/subjective terms present: etc.
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define a closed `LENSES: frozenset[str]` vocabulary in `registry.py` (mirroring `SOURCES`/`CONFIDENCES`) covering all registered values (`code_health`, `delivery`, `agent_process`, `gate_economics`) and reference it in the output-format spec.
evidence: Plan output schema: `{"lens","source","confidence","value"}`
registry.py defines `SOURCES: frozenset` and `CONFIDENCES: frozenset` but no `LENSES` frozenset
Registered lens values found across reader modules: `code_health` (registry.py, git_metrics.py), `delivery` (registry.py), `agent_process` (event_metrics.py), `gate_economics` (sidecar_metrics.py)
ABSENCE: no `LENSES` or lens vocabulary defined anywhere in src/rebar/metrics/
findings.finding: The `lens` field is included in the machine-contract JSON output schema (`{"lens","source","confidence","value"}`) and in every MetricSpec, but its value vocabulary is never defined. Unlike `source` (SOURCES frozenset) and `confidence` (CONFIDENCES frozenset), there is no closed `LENSES` vocabulary anywhere in registry.py or the metrics package — yet the actual registered values span `code_health`, `delivery`, `agent_process`, and `gate_economics`.
findings.location: ## Approach — Output formats section; src/rebar/metrics/registry.py (MetricSpec.lens: str)
scenarios: An implementer rendering the JSON output has no spec to validate/enumerate lens values, and a future reader module could silently introduce a novel lens string that breaks downstream consumers expecting a closed set.
The text-output renderer (`<id>  [<lens>/<source>]  <value>`) has no canonical list of lens values to document or test against.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to registry.py: `LENSES: frozenset[str] = frozenset({"code_health", "delivery", "agent_process", "gate_economics"})` alongside the existing SOURCES/CONFIDENCES frozensets, and note this vocabulary in the Output formats section.

findings.finding: The plan specifies that `metrics.py` constructs a context carrying `repo_root` ('the resolved store root') but never states how `metrics.py` resolves that root. All registered readers depend on this being the correct git-working-tree root: `git_metrics` scans `<repo_root>/src/rebar/**/*.py` and reads `<repo_root>/.github/module-size-limit.txt`; `event_metrics` calls `rebar.config.tracker_dir(repo_root)` to locate the ticket store. Without specifying the resolution mechanism (e.g., `config.repo_root()`, a `--repo-root` flag, or CWD), an implementer may use an incorrect value that silently produces empty/wrong results.
findings.location: Approach section: 'metrics.py constructs a small context (a dataclass or SimpleNamespace) carrying exactly repo_root (the resolved store root), since, and until'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Approach's context object paragraph: 'repo_root is resolved via `str(rebar.config.repo_root())` — the same call every other CLI command uses — so REBAR_ROOT env overrides and git-root detection work consistently.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add non-happy-path UX spec: define the exit code and error message for (a) invalid date format, (b) inverted date range, (c) invalid `--bucket` value, (d) invalid `--output` value, and (e) uninitialized store — and add at least one AC covering a bad-input path (e.g., invalid `--since` exits non-zero with a human-readable message).
evidence: AC only covers exit 0 and happy-path JSON structure; no AC for bad-input exit codes or error messages
Plan lists '--bucket day|week|month' but no validation or error message for out-of-enum values
'reads-with-init' grouping implies an init check but the error message/exit code on an uninitialized store is unspecified
No mention of argparse validation errors or date-parse failure messages
findings.finding: The plan specifies only the happy-path UX for `rebar metrics`: it covers exit 0 and well-formed JSON output, but does not specify behavior for invalid/missing inputs — invalid `--since/--until` date formats, inverted date range (`--since` > `--until`), unrecognized `--output` value, invalid `--bucket` value (e.g., `--bucket fortnight`), or running against an uninitialized store.
findings.location: Approach — 'Output formats' / Acceptance Criteria
scenarios: User types `rebar metrics --since yesterday --until today` (non-ISO format) — silent crash or confusing traceback
User types `--bucket fortnight` — argparse error or silent wrong behavior
User runs `rebar metrics` before `rebar init` — unclear error vs. generic crash
User supplies `--since 2026-07-01 --until 2026-01-01` (inverted) — undefined: empty output, error, or loop crash

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `tests/golden/cli_help/__overview_noargs.out` and `tests/golden/cli_help/__overview_dashh.out` to the regeneration edit set alongside the two already named overview snapshots.
evidence: tests/golden/cli_help/__overview_noargs.out — contains full subcommand list, confirmed by file read
tests/golden/cli_help/__overview_dashh.out — contains identical subcommand list, confirmed by file read
test_cli_argparse.py _OVERVIEW_FORMS = [('__overview_noargs', []), ('__overview_help', ['help']), ('__overview_dashhelp', ['--help']), ('__overview_dashh', ['-h']), ...]
All four call _help.overview() which reads overview.txt; adding a metrics line changes all four
findings.finding: Two of the four overview golden snapshot files that embed the subcommand list are missing from the plan's edit set: `__overview_noargs.out` and `__overview_dashh.out` also contain the full subcommand overview (identical content to `__overview_help.out`/`__overview_dashhelp.out`) and are pinned by the parametrized `test_help_byte_parity` test in `tests/interfaces/facades/test_cli_argparse.py`. Adding `metrics` to `overview.txt` without regenerating all four will cause those golden tests to fail.
findings.location: Files section / 'tests/golden/cli_help/*.out — regenerate the overview snapshots (__overview_help.out, __overview_dashhelp.out)'
scenarios: After implementing the plan, `pytest tests/interfaces/facades/test_cli_argparse.py` fails on `test_help_byte_parity[__overview_noargs]` and `test_help_byte_parity[__overview_dashh]` because those two goldens were not regenerated.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Files section, change 'regenerate the overview snapshots (`__overview_help.out`, `__overview_dashhelp.out`)' to 'regenerate all four overview snapshots (`__overview_noargs.out`, `__overview_help.out`, `__overview_dashhelp.out`, `__overview_dashh.out`)' — all four encode the subcommand list from `overview.txt`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate the full golden triplet for both help forms in the Files section: `metrics__help.out`, `metrics__help.err`, `metrics__help.exit`, `metrics__dashhelp.out`, `metrics__dashhelp.err`, `metrics__dashhelp.exit` (the `.err` and `.exit` companions are required by `test_cli_argparse.py::test_help_byte_parity`).
evidence: test_cli_argparse.py line 61: cases.append((f'{sub}__dashhelp', [sub, '--help']))
test_cli_argparse.py line 62: cases.append((f'{sub}__help', ['help', sub]))
test_cli_argparse.py _golden() reads .out, .err, AND .exit for each case
Plan only mentions 'metrics__help.out' — omits the .err/.exit companions and the full dashhelp triplet
findings.finding: `tests/interfaces/facades/test_cli_argparse.py` is an unenumerated existing consumer: its parametrized `test_help_byte_parity` test auto-expands to cover `metrics__help` and `metrics__dashhelp` as soon as `metrics.txt` is added (via `_all_subcommands()` → `known_subcommands()`), so two new golden files (`metrics__help.out/.err/.exit` and `metrics__dashhelp.out/.err/.exit`) must exist before the test suite runs. The plan's golden section only names the stem `metrics__help.out` without the `.err`/`.exit` triplet, and omits `metrics__dashhelp.*` entirely.
findings.location: Files section / 'tests/golden/cli_help/*.out — ... add `metrics__help.out`'
scenarios: After adding metrics.txt, pytest parametrizes two new cases (metrics__dashhelp, metrics__help) and tries to read metrics__dashhelp.out/.err/.exit and metrics__help.err/.exit — all of which are absent unless explicitly created, causing FileNotFoundError in test collection.

findings.finding: The plan only names two overview golden snapshots to regenerate (`__overview_help.out`, `__overview_dashhelp.out`) but the full overview text appears in at least three additional golden files (`__overview_noargs.out`, `__overview_dashh.out`, `__unknown__.out`) that also encode the full subcommand list and must be regenerated.
findings.location: Files section: `tests/golden/cli_help/*.out — regenerate the overview snapshots (__overview_help.out, __overview_dashhelp.out)`
findings.suggested_fix: In the Files section, change the golden entry to: `tests/golden/cli_help/*.out — regenerate ALL overview-containing snapshots: __overview_noargs.out, __overview_help.out, __overview_dashhelp.out, __overview_dashh.out, __unknown__.out, plus add metrics__help.out and metrics__dashhelp.out.`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a lightweight AC (or a note that docs are gated by CI doc-lint) verifying that `docs/cli-reference.md` contains the `metrics` subcommand entry after the change.
evidence: "docs/cli-reference.md — document the command" listed under Files
No AC references docs/cli-reference.md or verifies the metrics command is documented there.
findings.finding: `docs/cli-reference.md` is listed as an explicit deliverable in the Files section but no AC covers its update — it could be skipped or left stale with all ACs still passing.
findings.location: Files section ('docs/cli-reference.md — document the command'); Acceptance Criteria
scenarios: Implementer omits the docs update; all five ACs pass; the reference doc is never updated.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a note to `AGENTS.md` (or a new `docs/adr/metrics-registry-hydration.md`) capturing (a) the side-effect registration contract (`rebar/metrics/__init__.py` must import all reader modules), and (b) the adapter-isolation invariant enforced by the source-scan test, so both survive beyond the plan text.
evidence: "REGISTRY is only fully populated after the reader modules run their registration side-effects" — described inline in plan but not in any persistent design doc
Files list includes docs/cli-reference.md but no AGENTS.md or ADR entry
Adapter isolation references prior decisions '1f77/538c' but the new enforcement mechanism (source scan) has no doc home
ABSENCE: no AGENTS.md update in the Files list
findings.finding: The REGISTRY hydration pattern (importing the rebar.metrics *package* to trigger reader-registration side-effects) and the adapter-isolation enforcement mechanism (source-level AST/text scan in tests) are novel architectural decisions described only in the plan text — neither AGENTS.md nor an ADR is updated to capture them. A future contributor adding a new reader module who imports only `rebar.metrics.registry` will silently lose that metric with no guidance about why.
findings.location: Approach section — 'REGISTRY hydration' and 'Adapter isolation' paragraphs; no corresponding AGENTS.md / ADR update in Files list
scenarios: A new reader module added to the metrics package without updating rebar/metrics/__init__.py causes its metric to silently vanish from CLI output — the registration contract is nowhere documented for contributors
A future refactor removes the source-scan test without realising it enforces an architectural invariant — the adapter isolation guard quietly disappears
findings.suggested_fix: Add one section to AGENTS.md under a 'Metrics subsystem' heading: explain that `rebar.metrics.__init__` is the authoritative hydration point (new readers must be imported there), and that `rebar.metrics.adapters.*` must never be imported by core command modules (enforced by `test_metrics_cli.py`'s source scan).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add ACs (and corresponding test cases) for at least three failure paths: (a) malformed `--since`/`--until` exits non-zero with a descriptive message, (b) inverted date range is rejected or produces an empty/explained result, and (c) an unrecognised `--bucket` or `--output` value exits non-zero — covering the full user-facing contract, not just the golden path.
evidence: AC1 only exercises `--since 2026-01-01 --until 2026-07-01 --output json` (well-formed, happy path)
AC3 only asserts exit 0 and help contains `--since`
AC5 only asserts 'at least one bucket key'
ABSENCE: no AC or test mentions malformed date strings, `--since > --until`, invalid `--bucket` values, invalid `--output` values, or missing store
findings.finding: The testing plan covers only happy-path CLI invocations; no AC or test case addresses invalid/missing arguments (malformed --since/--until dates, inverted date range, unknown --bucket value, unknown --output value) or store-not-initialized failure, leaving the error-handling paths entirely untested for a new user-facing command.
findings.location: Tests section; Acceptance Criteria AC1, AC3, AC5
scenarios: User passes `--since not-a-date` — command may traceback instead of printing a clean error and exiting non-zero
User passes `--since 2026-07-01 --until 2026-01-01` — inverted range silently yields empty results or crashes
User passes `--bucket fortnight` — argparse may error or propagate garbage to the registry loop
CI pipeline catches none of these because the test suite only exercises valid inputs
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Acceptance Criteria: 'rebar metrics --since bad-date exits non-zero and stderr contains a date-parse error'; 'rebar metrics --since 2026-07-01 --until 2026-01-01 exits non-zero or emits an empty metrics map with a warning'; 'rebar metrics --bucket fortnight exits non-zero (argparse choices enforcement)'. Mirror these in the test module as parametrised subprocess assertions.

findings.finding: The plan asserts that `metrics.py` reaches a fully-populated REGISTRY by importing 'through `rebar.metrics.registry` (and the package import that populates REGISTRY)', but `rebar.metrics.registry` alone contains only the 3 seed specs with `_no_data_yet` computes. The git/event/sidecar metrics are only appended to REGISTRY as side-effects of importing `rebar.metrics` (the package `__init__.py` imports `event_metrics`, `git_metrics`, `sidecar_metrics` which each call `register()`). If `metrics.py` imports from `rebar.metrics.registry` directly without also importing `rebar.metrics` (or triggering the package `__init__`), it will receive an incomplete REGISTRY — and the AC test `set(output['metrics'].keys()) >= {s.id for s in REGISTRY}` would trivially pass against the truncated seed set rather than the full populated registry.
findings.location: Approach section — 'it builds a context object and calls rebar.metrics.registry.evaluate(spec, context) for every MetricSpec' / 'reaches metrics only through rebar.metrics.registry (and the package import that populates REGISTRY)'
findings.suggested_fix: In the Approach section, replace the parenthetical with: 'metrics.py imports `from rebar.metrics import REGISTRY, evaluate` (the package `__init__` triggers the reader side-effects); the AC test similarly imports REGISTRY via `from rebar.metrics import REGISTRY` so it sees the fully-populated set.'

findings.finding: The plan's Approach says metrics.py 'reaches metrics only through rebar.metrics.registry (and the package import that populates REGISTRY)', but this is ambiguous: importing rebar.metrics.registry alone does NOT trigger the register() side-effect calls in git_metrics, event_metrics, or sidecar_metrics — those are triggered only by importing the rebar.metrics package (__init__.py line 18). A developer who writes `from rebar.metrics.registry import REGISTRY, evaluate` will get only the 3 seed specs in REGISTRY, not the 9 reader-registered specs, making AC1 fail silently (the map will have only 3 entries, not all registered ids).
findings.location: Approach section, paragraph 1: '(and the package import that populates REGISTRY)'; AC1
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Approach: 'metrics.py must `import rebar.metrics` (the package __init__) before iterating REGISTRY, so that git_metrics.register(), event_metrics.register(), and sidecar_metrics.register() have fired and all specs are present. Importing only rebar.metrics.registry skips those side effects and leaves REGISTRY with only the 3 seed specs.'

findings.finding: The plan instructs `metrics.py` to import only `rebar.metrics.registry` (not `rebar.metrics`), but the full REGISTRY is only populated after `rebar.metrics.__init__` runs — which imports event_metrics, git_metrics, and sidecar_metrics as side effects. Importing `rebar.metrics.registry` directly yields only the 3 seed stubs, so the command would dispatch against an incomplete REGISTRY and the AC coverage assertion would silently validate only the stubs.
findings.location: Approach section: 'it builds a context object and calls rebar.metrics.registry.evaluate(spec, context) for every MetricSpec' / 'metrics.py reaches metrics only through rebar.metrics.registry'
findings.suggested_fix: Change the approach text to: 'metrics.py imports `rebar.metrics` (the package) so that __init__.py triggers event_metrics/git_metrics/sidecar_metrics registrations as side effects; it then iterates over `rebar.metrics.registry.REGISTRY`. It must NOT import rebar.metrics.adapters — the source-level guard remains.'

findings.finding: The REGISTRY hydration import protocol is under-specified: the plan says `metrics.py` imports through `rebar.metrics.registry` but the full REGISTRY (including event/git/sidecar metrics) is only populated when `rebar.metrics` (the package `__init__`) is imported, not `rebar.metrics.registry` alone — an implementor following the plan's wording may import only the registry module and get a 3-entry stub REGISTRY.
findings.location: Approach section: 'reaches metrics only through `rebar.metrics.registry` (and the package import that populates `REGISTRY`)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Approach: 'metrics.py must `import rebar.metrics` (not just `rebar.metrics.registry`) so that event_metrics/git_metrics/sidecar_metrics register() side-effects run and REGISTRY is fully hydrated before the render loop iterates it.'

findings.finding: The plan instructs the implementer to 'register the `metrics` read subcommand in the command-set frozensets' in `_cli/__init__.py`, but no existing frozenset accommodates a novel command with a custom handler: every frozenset routes to a shared backend (`reads.main`, `commands_main`, etc.), and adding `metrics` to any of them (e.g. `_READS_INIT_ONLY`) would route it to that wrong backend. The correct mechanism is a direct `if sub == 'metrics':` arm in `_dispatch()` (matching how `audit`, `config`, `grounding-info` etc. are handled), not a frozenset entry.
findings.location: Files section: `src/rebar/_cli/__init__.py` — 'register the metrics read subcommand in the command-set frozensets and dispatch'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'register the `metrics` read subcommand in the command-set frozensets' with: 'add a direct `if sub == "metrics":` arm in `_dispatch()` that imports and calls `rebar._commands.metrics.metrics_cli(rest)` — matching the pattern used by `fsck`, `delete`, and other novel commands rather than using an existing frozenset.'

findings.finding: The plan says 'register the `metrics` read subcommand in the command-set frozensets' but the existing dispatch architecture enforces a three-way invariant tested by `test_help_overview_coverage.py`: every subcommand must be (a) in a routable frozenset OR an explicit if-arm listed in `_routable_subcommands()`, (b) have a `help/<sub>.txt` file, AND (c) appear in `overview.txt`. The plan addresses (b) and (c) explicitly, but for (a) it only says 'frozensets' without specifying which one — and the analogous read-only command `grounding-info` is actually dispatched via a named intercept arm (`if sub == 'grounding-info'`), NOT via any frozenset. Placing `metrics` in the wrong frozenset (e.g. `_READS_INIT_ONLY`, which calls `reads.main`) would route it to the wrong handler.
findings.location: Files section — `src/rebar/_cli/__init__.py` — 'register the metrics read subcommand in the command-set frozensets and dispatch'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either (a) seed a minimal event record into the temp store before the `--bucket week` invocation so at least one metric has a non-`unavailable` value, and assert that the per-bucket `metrics` values differ across buckets, OR (b) tighten the structural assertion to verify the number of buckets matches the expected subdivision of the date range (e.g. a 14-day range with `--bucket week` produces exactly 2 bucket entries).
evidence: AC5: 'CLI test asserts the JSON contains at least one bucket key'
Tests section: 'CLI (real subprocess against a temp store)' — no mention of seeding event/git history before the --bucket invocation
Approach: 'an unpopulated metric shows `unavailable`' — the general test fixture is explicitly unpopulated
findings.finding: AC5 states the CLI test asserts 'the JSON contains at least one bucket key' for 'a metric with history', but the described test fixture is an unpopulated temp store. With no history, every metric will be `unavailable` — the assertion still passes because bucket structure exists regardless of metric values. The phrase 'a metric with history' implies real data should be present, but no setup step seeds history into the temp store, making the fixture invalid for the stated intent.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC5
scenarios: An implementation that emits bucket keys but returns all-`unavailable` values for every bucket passes AC5 even if the bucketing logic is broken (e.g., all buckets share the same sub-range by mistake).
findings.suggested_fix: Replace AC5 with: '`rebar metrics --since 2026-01-01 --until 2026-01-15 --bucket week --output json` exits 0 and `jq -e ".buckets | length == 2"` passes, confirming the range is subdivided into the correct number of weekly buckets.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the `--bucket` behavior for non-ranged metrics (e.g. emit them once at the top level outside the bucket series, or annotate them with `"bucketed": false`, or skip them from per-bucket output) so the machine contract is unambiguous for consumers.
evidence: `git_metrics._spec(..., ranged=False)` — compute adapter at line 247: `return fn(repo_root)` — ignores since/until entirely
Plan states: 'for `--bucket`, it builds one context per bucket sub-range and evaluates the registry per bucket, keying the output by bucket' — no exception or note for non-ranged metrics
AC for --bucket: 'JSON contains at least one bucket key' — does not address metric-level bucket semantics
findings.finding: The `--bucket` mechanism evaluates the full REGISTRY once per sub-range, but `git_metrics.module_size_distribution` is registered with `ranged=False` — its context adapter ignores `since`/`until` entirely and always returns a point-in-time working-tree snapshot. Under `--bucket week`, this metric produces the identical value for every bucket key, which is misleading (each bucket's output looks like independently-measured data). The plan's output contract and AC for `--bucket` do not specify the expected behavior for non-ranged metrics in a bucketed response.
findings.location: Approach section — '--bucket day|week|month splits the [since, until] range' / Output formats section
scenarios: User runs `rebar metrics --since 2026-01-01 --until 2026-07-01 --bucket week --output json` and sees `module_size_distribution` with the same value in all 26 weekly buckets, with no indication that it is a point-in-time metric rather than a per-week measurement

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether `metrics.py` will reuse `rebar._engine_support.output.parse_output` (the codebase-wide single source of truth for `--output` flag parsing) and, if JSON-as-default is intentional, add an explicit justification for deviating from the established `report` profile convention (which defaults to text).
evidence: Plan: 'JSON (default) is the machine contract' and '--output text is a deterministic human summary'
Plan: 'NOT borrowed from `rebar audit`, whose schema is structurally different'
rebar._engine_support.output.PROFILES['report'] = ('text', ('text', 'json')) — the established report profile defaults to text, not json

Every existing rebar command using --output (audit, validate, bridge-status, fsck, signing, gates, bridge-fsck, field_reads, grounding-info) imports and uses parse_output from rebar._engine_support.output
The plan contains no mention of parse_output or rebar._engine_support.output — the single source of truth would be bypassed
findings.finding: The plan introduces a bespoke `--output json|text` flag handling in `metrics.py` with JSON as the default, silently bypassing `rebar._engine_support.output.parse_output` — the codebase's single source of truth for `--output` flag parsing used by every other command — without justifying this novel departure from the established `report` profile pattern (which defaults to text, not json).
findings.location: ## Approach — Output formats section; ## Acceptance Criteria AC1 (`--output json`)
scenarios: An implementer following the plan literally rolls a new --output parser in metrics.py, duplicating the accepted-spellings logic (-o json / --output=json / --output json) already in output.py, creating a second source of truth and inconsistent error messages.

The JSON-default breaks user expectation set by every other report-style command (validate, bridge-status, fsck, etc. all default to text); a human running `rebar metrics --since ... --until ...` gets raw JSON instead of a readable summary, with no prior-art command in rebar having this behavior.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Approach section, add: 'Output-flag parsing uses `rebar._engine_support.output.parse_output` with a custom profile `allowed=("json", "text"), default="json"` (JSON-first is intentional for this machine-contract command; differs from the `report` profile which is text-first).' This documents the reuse of the shared infrastructure and explicitly justifies the JSON-default divergence.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define and document the bucket sub-range splitting convention (week start day, partial-bucket handling at range edges) in either the help text or a code comment, and extract the splitting logic into a named utility function so it can be adjusted or extended without touching the render loop.
evidence: "--bucket day|week|month splits the [since, until] range into sub-ranges" — no definition of week-start convention or month-end edge-case handling
No configurable locale/calendar parameter or reference to a shared calendar utility
ABSENCE: no mention of what happens when `--since` falls mid-week or the range does not divide evenly into buckets
findings.finding: The `--bucket` sub-range splitting logic (day/week/month) embeds calendar-convention rules (e.g., week start day, month-boundary handling) that are asserted in the plan without specifying whether they are configurable or at least documented as a fixed convention. These rules are expected to evolve (locale differences, fiscal calendars, off-by-one at range boundaries) but the plan treats them as implementation details with no changeability provision.
findings.location: Approach section — '--bucket day|week|month splits the [since, until] range into sub-ranges'; AC5 — '--bucket week yields a per-bucket series'
scenarios: A user running `rebar metrics --bucket week --since 2026-01-01 --until 2026-01-15` gets surprising bucket boundaries because Monday-vs-Sunday convention is hardcoded and undocumented
A later ticket tries to support fiscal-quarter buckets and the splitting logic is tightly coupled inside metrics.py with no extraction point

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend the CLI test) that exercises `--output text`, asserts exit 0, and verifies the per-metric line format (including an `unavailable: <reason>` line for an uncomputable metric).
evidence: "'--output text' is a deterministic human summary — one line per metric in registry order: <id>  [<lens>/<source>]  <value-or-'unavailable: <reason>'>"
No AC references --output text or verifies the text rendering path.
findings.finding: The `--output text` format is a fully described deliverable (with its own schema: one line per metric, fixed-width layout) but no AC covers it — no test asserts text output exits 0, produces the correct per-metric lines, or renders `unavailable` correctly in that format.
findings.location: Approach → 'Output formats' subsection; Acceptance Criteria (all five ACs)
scenarios: An implementer ships broken text output (wrong columns, missing unavailable rendering) and all ACs still pass because no test exercises that path.
findings.suggested_fix: AC6: `rebar metrics --since 2026-01-01 --until 2026-07-01 --output text` exits 0 and stdout contains one line per registered metric id, each matching `<id>  [<lens>/<source>]  <value|unavailable: ...>`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the source-grep AC2 with a behavioral isolation test: e.g., assert that importing `rebar._commands.metrics` (or running `rebar metrics`) does NOT cause any `rebar.metrics.adapters.*` module to appear in `sys.modules`, which verifies the runtime contract rather than source text.
evidence: Plan states: 'the test reads src/rebar/_commands/metrics.py and asserts its source contains no rebar.metrics.adapters import (a text/AST check)'
AC2: 'a test asserts src/rebar/_commands/metrics.py does not import rebar.metrics.adapters'
This is explicitly a source-grep, not a behavioral assertion about what the command imports or executes at runtime
findings.finding: AC2 (the portability/adapter-isolation guard) is a source-code text/AST scan that checks whether `metrics.py` contains the string `rebar.metrics.adapters` — this is a structural grep / change-detector anti-pattern masquerading as a behavioral test; it verifies source layout rather than runtime isolation, locks in the current source text, and can be trivially bypassed (e.g., via `importlib.import_module`).
findings.location: Approach section ('Adapter isolation (portability guard)'); Acceptance Criteria AC2
scenarios: A developer refactors the import to use `importlib.import_module('rebar.metrics.adapters.foo')` — the source grep passes, isolation is broken, but the test goes green
The test is tied to current file layout; a rename or restructure causes a false failure with no behavioral change
The anti-pattern locks in source text rather than enforcing the architectural property
findings.suggested_fix: In the test, after `import rebar._commands.metrics`, assert `not any(k.startswith('rebar.metrics.adapters') for k in sys.modules)`. This is a true behavioral check of import-time isolation that cannot be bypassed by refactoring the import form.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
